function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
The AI segment generated $3,201M of FY2025 revenue. Identify which AI sub-components are disclosed with revenue granularity and which are not. What does the level of disclosure permit and not permit an analyst to conclude about Grok monetization?

steps.reasoning_content:
The user is asking about the SpaceX S-1 filing, specifically about the AI segment's FY2025 revenue of $3,201M. I need to identify which AI sub-components are disclosed with revenue granularity and which are not, and what conclusions an analyst can and cannot draw about Grok monetization.

Let me start by parsing the SpaceX S-1 filing and then retrieving information about the AI segment revenue.

results.content: SUCCESS: Loaded portable assets for key: spacex_s1

arguments.prompt: What are the AI segment revenue sub-components disclosed in {{spacex_s1}}? Specifically, I need to know what revenue line items or sub-categories are disclosed within the AI segment for FY2025, including any breakdown by product (Grok, API, enterprise, consumer subscriptions, advertising, etc.). Also identify what is NOT disclosed with revenue granularity.

results.content:
# AI Segment Revenue Sub-Components Disclosed for FY2025

## Top-Level Disaggregation (from the Revenue Table)

The AI segment total revenue of **$3,201 million** for FY2025 is disaggregated into two disclosed line items:

| AI Sub-Component | FY2025 | FY2024 | FY2023 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Advertising** | $1,844 | $1,728 | $2,323 |
| **AI Solutions & Infrastructure** | $1,357 | $892 | $638 |
| **AI (Total)** | $3,201 | $2,620 | $2,961 |

## Qualitative Sub-Components of "AI Solutions & Infrastructure"

The notes describe three revenue streams within AI Solutions & Infrastructure, but **do not report separate dollar figures for each in the tables**:

1. **Premium subscriptions on X and Grok** — recognized ratably over the subscription term (month-to-month up to one year)
2. **Data licensing arrangements** — recognized ratably over the period (month-to-month up to two years)
3. **API access to Grok models** — recognized ratably over the contract term (month-to-month up to one year) for stand-ready access, or as consumed for usage-based arrangements

## Incremental Granularity from MD&A (YoY Change Disclosures Only)

The MD&A provides **change amounts** (not absolute balances) for FY2025 vs. FY2024:

- Advertising revenue **increased $116 million**
- AI Solutions & Infrastructure revenue **increased $465 million**, driven by:
  - X and Grok subscription revenue: **+$365 million**
  - Data licensing arrangements: **+$88 million**
  - (API access change not separately quantified)

## What Is NOT Disclosed with Revenue Granularity

| Item Not Disclosed | Notes |
|---|---|
| **Absolute dollar values for subscriptions, data licensing, and API access separately** | Only the aggregate "AI Solutions & Infrastructure" figure appears in the table; MD&A gives only YoY deltas for two of the three |
| **API access revenue** (absolute or change) | Mentioned qualitatively but never quantified, even as a YoY change |
| **X subscriptions vs. Grok subscriptions split** | Always combined as "X and Grok subscription revenue" |
| **Consumer vs. enterprise split within AI** | No customer-type revenue breakdown for the AI segment (unlike Connectivity, which splits Consumer vs. Enterprise & Government) |
| **Advertising sub-categories** (e.g., display, video, sponsored) | No breakdown beyond the single "Advertising" line |
| **Geographic revenue breakdown for AI** | Not provided |
| **Principal vs. agent (gross vs. net) revenue split for advertising** | Policy is described (gross for direct X platform sales; net for SSP-partner sales), but no dollar split is disclosed |
| **Products vs. services split within AI** | The preamble notes "All of products revenue is attributable to the Connectivity segment," implying AI is entirely services, but no explicit AI products/services table line is shown |
| **Grok-specific standalone revenue** | Never isolated; always bundled with X subscriptions |
| **Revenue by individual contract or customer** | Not disclosed |

## Summary

The AI segment is disclosed at **two levels of revenue granularity in the financial tables**: Advertising and AI Solutions & Infrastructure. A **third qualitative level** (subscriptions, data licensing, API access) is described in the revenue recognition policy but is **not quantified in the tables**. The MD&A partially bridges this gap by disclosing YoY change amounts for subscriptions and data licensing (but not API access), and only for the most recent annual and quarterly comparisons.

steps.reasoning_content: I have a comprehensive understanding of the AI segment revenue disclosure. Let me also verify the MD&A discussion of Grok monetization specifically to ensure I capture all relevant disclosures.

arguments.prompt: In {{spacex_s1}}, what does the MD&A or risk factors say about Grok monetization, Grok subscription revenue, Grok API access, and any forward-looking statements about Grok's commercialization? Also, what does the filing say about the relationship between X subscriptions and Grok subscriptions, and whether Grok is bundled or sold separately?

results.content:
Based on the provided filing sections, here is what the document says regarding Grok’s monetization, subscription revenue, API access, commercialization risks, and its relationship to X subscriptions:

### Grok Monetization and Subscription Revenue (MD&A)
The MD&A sections highlight that Grok is a rapidly growing driver of the company’s AI solutions and infrastructure revenue, often reported alongside X subscriptions:
*   **Fiscal Year 2025:** Revenue increased by $581 million (22.2%) compared to 2024, driven largely by a $365 million increase in X and Grok subscription revenue.
*   **Q1 2026:** AI solutions and infrastructure revenue increased by $191 million, primarily due to a $177 million increase in Grok and X subscription revenue. 
*   **Cost of Revenue:** The increases in cost of revenue for both 2025 and Q1 2026 were partially driven by higher infrastructure, cloud computing costs, revenue share expenses, and payment processing fees attributable to this increased subscriber revenue.

### Grok API Access
The filing details that API access is a distinct revenue stream for the AI segment, alongside subscriptions and data licensing:
*   **Revenue Recognition:** Revenue from providing API access to Grok models is recognized ratably over the contract term (typically month-to-month or up to one year) for stand-ready access, or as services are consumed for usage-based arrangements.
*   **Advertising:** Currently, Grok API access is *not* included in advertising rates, and the company does not currently sell or offer advertisers the ability to place ads via the API.
*   **Availability:** Grok Chat is available to users via the xAI API, as well as grok.com, dedicated mobile apps, and X platform integration.

### Forward-Looking Statements and Commercialization (Risk Factors & Strategy)
The filing balances an aggressive commercialization strategy with explicit warnings about the risks of the AI market:
*   **Market Risks:** The AI industry is "highly dynamic and rapidly evolving." The company faces significant uncertainty regarding technological developments, changing customer preferences, evolving regulations, and intense competition. Consequently, download and usage metrics for Grok can "fluctuate significantly," including periods of decreased app downloads.
*   **Model Velocity as a Strategy:** To drive commercialization, the company highlights its "exceptional model velocity," noting it develops leading frontier models faster than competitors. The roadmap includes multi-trillion parameter models (like Grok 5) to increase reasoning depth and drive long-term subscriber growth.
*   **Future Monetization Avenues:** The company believes it is still early in increasing paid penetration across the Grok user base. It also sees an "incremental monetization opportunity by introducing advertising into our stand-alone Grok offering."
*   **Enterprise/Government Commercialization:** The company offers tailored deployments of Grok for enterprises via "Grok Business and Grok Enterprise," sold on license-, consumption-, or outcome-based pricing models. It also cites its government contractor track record as a structural advantage as governments become major AI consumers.

### Relationship Between X Subscriptions and Grok Subscriptions
The filing treats X and Grok subscriptions as complementary pillars of a unified strategy to convert free users into paying subscribers:
*   **Combined Financial Reporting:** While tracked together as "X and Grok subscription revenue" in the MD&A, the preamble breaks down the specific subscriber metrics as of March 31, 2026: 
    *   **Total active paid subscribers:** ~6.3 million
    *   **X Premium and Premium+ paid subscribers:** ~4.4 million
    *   **SuperGrok, SuperGrok Heavy, and SuperGrok Lite paid subscribers:** ~1.9 million
*   **Shared Value Proposition:** Both subscription types offer enhanced functionality, exclusive features, and access to the latest AI models. The company plans to continue adding features to both to increase the penetration rate of the combined subscriber base.

### Bundled vs. Sold Separately
Based on the text, Grok subscriptions and X subscriptions are **sold as separate, distinct tiers**, but are deeply integrated:
*   **Distinct Tiers:** The filing explicitly lists separate subscription offerings: X subscriptions (Premium and Premium+) and Grok subscriptions (basic, SuperGrok, SuperGrok Heavy, and SuperGrok Lite). The separate subscriber counts (4.4M vs 1.9M) further indicate these are distinct purchasing paths.
*   **Platform Integration:** Despite being sold separately, Grok is heavily integrated into the X ecosystem. The company intends to "further embed Grok throughout the platform to enhance discovery, analysis of posts, user support, and personalization, making core workflows more useful and reducing friction for users to adopt paid features." 
*   **Standalone Grok:** The text explicitly refers to a "stand-alone Grok offering," which can be accessed via grok.com and dedicated mobile apps, separate from the X platform.

steps.message: I have sufficient information to answer the question. Let me compile the final answer.
steps.reasoning_content:
I now have a comprehensive understanding of the AI segment revenue disclosure. Let me also verify the exact figures and check if there's any additional granularity I might have missed. Let me also confirm the FY2025 revenue figure of $3,201M.

Based on my research:

**Disclosed with revenue granularity (in financial tables):**
1. Advertising: $1,844M
2. AI Solutions & Infrastructure: $1,357M
3. AI Total: $3,201M

**Disclosed qualitatively but NOT with absolute dollar granularity:**
- Premium subscriptions on X and Grok (combined)
- Data licensing arrangements
- API access to Grok models

**MD&A provides YoY change amounts (not absolute):**
- X and Grok subscription revenue: +$365M
- Data licensing arrangements: +$88M
- API access: not separately quantified

**What is NOT disclosed:**
- Absolute dollar values for subscriptions, data licensing, and API access separately
- API access revenue (absolute or change)
- X subscriptions vs. Grok subscriptions split
- Consumer vs. enterprise split within AI
- Advertising sub-categories
- Geographic revenue breakdown for AI
- Grok-specific standalone revenue

**Subscriber metrics disclosed (as of March 31, 2026):**
- Total active paid subscribers: ~6.3 million
- X Premium and Premium+ paid subscribers: ~4.4 million
- SuperGrok, SuperGrok Heavy, and SuperGrok Lite paid subscribers: ~1.9 million

Now let me think about what an analyst can and cannot conclude about Grok monetization:

**What an analyst CAN conclude:**
1. Grok is bundled with X subscriptions in the financial reporting - cannot be isolated
2. The combined X and Grok subscription revenue grew $365M YoY in FY2025
3. Grok has ~1.9M paid subscribers (SuperGrok tiers) as of March 31, 2026
4. Grok is sold both as standalone (grok.com, mobile apps) and integrated with X
5. API access to Grok is a distinct revenue stream but not quantified
6. The company sees future monetization opportunities (advertising in standalone Grok, enterprise/government deployments)
7. Grok is a rapidly growing driver of AI Solutions & Infrastructure revenue

**What an analyst CANNOT conclude:**
1. The absolute dollar amount of Grok-specific revenue (it's always bundled with X)
2. The absolute dollar amount of API access revenue
3. The split between X subscriptions and Grok subscriptions in dollar terms
4. ARPU for Grok specifically (since revenue is bundled)
5. The contribution of Grok to the $1,357M AI Solutions & Infrastructure total
6. Whether Grok is profitable on a standalone basis
7. The growth rate of Grok-specific revenue (only the combined growth is disclosed)
8. The relative contribution of consumer vs. enterprise Grok revenue

Let me now formulate my final answer.

arguments.final_result:
## AI Segment Revenue Disclosure Analysis — SpaceX S-1 (Filed May 20, 2026)

The AI segment's FY2025 revenue of **$3,201M** is presented in the consolidated revenue table at **two levels of dollar granularity**, with a third qualitative level described in the revenue-recognition policy but not quantified in the tables.

### 1. AI Sub-Components Disclosed WITH Revenue Granularity

| AI Sub-Component | FY2025 | FY2024 | FY2023 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Advertising | $1,844M | $1,728M | $2,323M |
| AI Solutions & Infrastructure | $1,357M | $892M | $638M |
| **AI (Total)** | **$3,201M** | **$2,620M** | **$2,961M** |

The MD&A additionally discloses **year-over-year change amounts** (not absolute balances) for two of the three sub-streams within "AI Solutions & Infrastructure":
- X and Grok subscription revenue: **+$365M** YoY
- Data licensing arrangements: **+$88M** YoY
- (API access change is not separately quantified)

### 2. AI Sub-Components Disclosed WITHOUT Revenue Granularity

The revenue-recognition policy describes three streams inside "AI Solutions & Infrastructure," but **only the aggregate $1,357M is reported**; none of the three is broken out as an absolute dollar figure:

1. **Premium subscriptions on X and Grok** — recognized ratably (month-to-month up to one year)
2. **Data licensing arrangements** — recognized ratably (month-to-month up to two years)
3. **API access to Grok models** — recognized ratably for stand-ready access, or as consumed for usage-based arrangements

The following are **not disclosed at any dollar level**:
- Absolute dollar values for subscriptions, data licensing, and API access separately
- **API access revenue** — never quantified, even as a YoY change
- **X subscriptions vs. Grok subscriptions split** — always combined as "X and Grok subscription revenue"
- Consumer vs. enterprise split within AI (unlike Connectivity, which splits Consumer vs. Enterprise & Government)
- Advertising sub-categories (display, video, sponsored, etc.)
- Geographic revenue breakdown for AI
- Gross vs. net (principal vs. agent) dollar split for advertising
- **Grok-specific standalone revenue** — never isolated

### 3. What the Disclosure PERMITS an Analyst to Conclude About Grok Monetization

- **Grok is a material and rapidly growing driver of AI Solutions & Infrastructure revenue.** The MD&A explicitly attributes the $465M YoY increase in AI Solutions & Infrastructure primarily to a $365M increase in "X and Grok subscription revenue," and Q1 2026 shows a further $177M increase in "Grok and X subscription revenue."
- **Grok has a measurable paid subscriber base.** As of March 31, 2026, the filing discloses ~6.3M total active paid subscribers, of which ~1.9M are SuperGrok / SuperGrok Heavy / SuperGrok Lite subscribers (the remainder ~4.4M are X Premium / Premium+).
- **Grok is monetized through multiple distinct channels** — consumer subscriptions (sold both standalone via grok.com/mobile apps and integrated into X), API access, and enterprise/government deployments (Grok Business / Grok Enterprise on license-, consumption-, or outcome-based pricing).
- **Grok is sold as a distinct product tier**, not merely a feature of X — the separate subscriber counts and the reference to a "stand-alone Grok offering" confirm this.
- **Future monetization avenues are flagged as opportunities, not realized revenue**: introducing advertising into the standalone Grok offering, and increasing paid penetration across the Grok user base. These are forward-looking and should not be treated as historical outcomes.
- **Cost structure is partially attributable to Grok**: higher cost of revenue in FY2025 and Q1 2026 was partially driven by infrastructure, cloud computing, revenue-share, and payment-processing fees tied to increased subscriber revenue.

### 4. What the Disclosure Does NOT Permit an Analyst to Conclude About Grok Monetization

- **Grok's absolute standalone revenue cannot be derived.** Grok subscription revenue is always bundled with X subscription revenue; no dollar split is provided. An analyst cannot compute Grok's contribution to the $1,357M AI Solutions & Infrastructure total.
- **Grok-specific ARPU cannot be computed.** With ~1.9M Grok subscribers disclosed but Grok revenue not isolated, average revenue per Grok subscriber is not derivable from the filing.
- **Grok-specific growth rate cannot be computed.** Only the combined "X and Grok" YoY change (+$365M) is disclosed; the Grok-only growth rate is unknown.
- **API access revenue — including Grok API revenue — is unquantified.** The filing confirms Grok API access is a distinct stream and that the company does not currently sell advertising via the API, but provides no dollar figure, so the size of the developer/API monetization channel is opaque.
- **Consumer vs. enterprise Grok revenue split is not disclosed.** Although Grok Business / Grok Enterprise are described qualitatively, their revenue contribution is not separable.
- **Grok standalone profitability cannot be assessed.** Cost of revenue increases are attributed to subscriber growth generally, but no Grok-specific cost or margin disclosure exists.
- **The relative weight of Grok vs. X within the combined subscription line is unknown.** An analyst cannot infer whether Grok or X is the larger contributor to the +$365M increase.

### 5. Bottom Line

The S-1 discloses AI revenue at a **two-line granularity** (Advertising; AI Solutions & Infrastructure) in the financial statements, with a **third qualitative layer** (subscriptions, data licensing, API access) described in policy but quantified only partially via YoY deltas in MD&A. Grok is **always reported bundled with X subscriptions**, and **API access is never quantified**. Consequently, an analyst can confirm that Grok is a fast-growing, multi-channel monetization engine with a disclosed ~1.9M paid subscriber base, but **cannot isolate Grok's absolute revenue, ARPU, growth rate, or profitability** from the filing as filed. Any Grok-specific revenue figure would require inference or external data not contained in the S-1.

---

  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm",
      "name": "SpaceX S-1 Registration Statement (filed May 20, 2026) — Consolidated Revenue Table, Revenue Recognition Policy, and MD&A"
    }
  ]
}

results.content:
## AI Segment Revenue Disclosure Analysis — SpaceX S-1 (Filed May 20, 2026)

The AI segment's FY2025 revenue of **$3,201M** is presented in the consolidated revenue table at **two levels of dollar granularity**, with a third qualitative level described in the revenue-recognition policy but not quantified in the tables.

### 1. AI Sub-Components Disclosed WITH Revenue Granularity

| AI Sub-Component | FY2025 | FY2024 | FY2023 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Advertising | $1,844M | $1,728M | $2,323M |
| AI Solutions & Infrastructure | $1,357M | $892M | $638M |
| **AI (Total)** | **$3,201M** | **$2,620M** | **$2,961M** |

The MD&A additionally discloses **year-over-year change amounts** (not absolute balances) for two of the three sub-streams within "AI Solutions & Infrastructure":
- X and Grok subscription revenue: **+$365M** YoY
- Data licensing arrangements: **+$88M** YoY
- (API access change is not separately quantified)

### 2. AI Sub-Components Disclosed WITHOUT Revenue Granularity

The revenue-recognition policy describes three streams inside "AI Solutions & Infrastructure," but **only the aggregate $1,357M is reported**; none of the three is broken out as an absolute dollar figure:

1. **Premium subscriptions on X and Grok** — recognized ratably (month-to-month up to one year)
2. **Data licensing arrangements** — recognized ratably (month-to-month up to two years)
3. **API access to Grok models** — recognized ratably for stand-ready access, or as consumed for usage-based arrangements

The following are **not disclosed at any dollar level**:
- Absolute dollar values for subscriptions, data licensing, and API access separately
- **API access revenue** — never quantified, even as a YoY change
- **X subscriptions vs. Grok subscriptions split** — always combined as "X and Grok subscription revenue"
- Consumer vs. enterprise split within AI (unlike Connectivity, which splits Consumer vs. Enterprise & Government)
- Advertising sub-categories (display, video, sponsored, etc.)
- Geographic revenue breakdown for AI
- Gross vs. net (principal vs. agent) dollar split for advertising
- **Grok-specific standalone revenue** — never isolated

### 3. What the Disclosure PERMITS an Analyst to Conclude About Grok Monetization

- **Grok is a material and rapidly growing driver of AI Solutions & Infrastructure revenue.** The MD&A explicitly attributes the $465M YoY increase in AI Solutions & Infrastructure primarily to a $365M increase in "X and Grok subscription revenue," and Q1 2026 shows a further $177M increase in "Grok and X subscription revenue."
- **Grok has a measurable paid subscriber base.** As of March 31, 2026, the filing discloses ~6.3M total active paid subscribers, of which ~1.9M are SuperGrok / SuperGrok Heavy / SuperGrok Lite subscribers (the remainder ~4.4M are X Premium / Premium+).
- **Grok is monetized through multiple distinct channels** — consumer subscriptions (sold both standalone via grok.com/mobile apps and integrated into X), API access, and enterprise/government deployments (Grok Business / Grok Enterprise on license-, consumption-, or outcome-based pricing).
- **Grok is sold as a distinct product tier**, not merely a feature of X — the separate subscriber counts and the reference to a "stand-alone Grok offering" confirm this.
- **Future monetization avenues are flagged as opportunities, not realized revenue**: introducing advertising into the standalone Grok offering, and increasing paid penetration across the Grok user base. These are forward-looking and should not be treated as historical outcomes.
- **Cost structure is partially attributable to Grok**: higher cost of revenue in FY2025 and Q1 2026 was partially driven by infrastructure, cloud computing, revenue-share, and payment-processing fees tied to increased subscriber revenue.

### 4. What the Disclosure Does NOT Permit an Analyst to Conclude About Grok Monetization

- **Grok's absolute standalone revenue cannot be derived.** Grok subscription revenue is always bundled with X subscription revenue; no dollar split is provided. An analyst cannot compute Grok's contribution to the $1,357M AI Solutions & Infrastructure total.
- **Grok-specific ARPU cannot be computed.** With ~1.9M Grok subscribers disclosed but Grok revenue not isolated, average revenue per Grok subscriber is not derivable from the filing.
- **Grok-specific growth rate cannot be computed.** Only the combined "X and Grok" YoY change (+$365M) is disclosed; the Grok-only growth rate is unknown.
- **API access revenue — including Grok API revenue — is unquantified.** The filing confirms Grok API access is a distinct stream and that the company does not currently sell advertising via the API, but provides no dollar figure, so the size of the developer/API monetization channel is opaque.
- **Consumer vs. enterprise Grok revenue split is not disclosed.** Although Grok Business / Grok Enterprise are described qualitatively, their revenue contribution is not separable.
- **Grok standalone profitability cannot be assessed.** Cost of revenue increases are attributed to subscriber growth generally, but no Grok-specific cost or margin disclosure exists.
- **The relative weight of Grok vs. X within the combined subscription line is unknown.** An analyst cannot infer whether Grok or X is the larger contributor to the +$365M increase.

### 5. Bottom Line

The S-1 discloses AI revenue at a **two-line granularity** (Advertising; AI Solutions & Infrastructure) in the financial statements, with a **third qualitative layer** (subscriptions, data licensing, API access) described in policy but quantified only partially via YoY deltas in MD&A. Grok is **always reported bundled with X subscriptions**, and **API access is never quantified**. Consequently, an analyst can confirm that Grok is a fast-growing, multi-channel monetization engine with a disclosed ~1.9M paid subscriber base, but **cannot isolate Grok's absolute revenue, ARPU, growth rate, or profitability** from the filing as filed. Any Grok-specific revenue figure would require inference or external data not contained in the S-1.
